sentences.text: Well, I don't recall specific items about Hitler's life and so on.
But I do recall Hitler as being a powerful presence that hung over everything.
I remember hearing his speeches on the radio, hearing his harangues.
He seemed to permeate all of life in school and out of school.
The Hitler speeches seemed to be there.
I don't recall sort of the formal content of what we were taught in school, except it was sort of heroic German history is the theme that seems to come out.
But also the sort of-- the poisonous fact that a Jewish child is in the same context as the Christian children being indoctrinated with anti-Semitism.
The same dose came at all of us together.
And it was very difficult to separate the message that was intended for Christians from that for Jews.
Jews were pictured increasingly as the enemy, the arch enemy of Germany.
And here we little children sat and had to eat it up that we were the enemy, the Jews were the enemy.
And so the indoctrination took place.
But in-- of everybody, Christians and Jews.
But it was very subtle and there was no clear distinction made between Jews and Christians.
No.
Well, there was one boy who was one year older than I who was closest to me.
I wouldn't say we were close friends.
I-- I used to play with the girl next door who was Jewish who was about three or four years younger than I, was not in class with me.
She was not yet-- I don't think she was yet in school for most of the time that I was there.
So I would play with her, the girl next door.
But I don't remember sort of playing interactions with other schoolmates, although much later-- I'm going to jump ahead for a second.
When I went back for my one visit to Germany in 1974, a man came up to me and said, don't you remember me?
We were the smallest ones in class and we always sat together.
And I didn't remember him.
I didn't tell him, but I did not remember him.
But he claims we sat together and had some kind of a friendship, although my memory of all the interactions with the German children were of terror.
And the overwhelming feeling I have about the school situation was fear, fear of being alive, fear of being beaten up, fear for my life, all the time.
Well, my father read newspapers.
I didn't.
I was 11 when I left eventually.
My father read newspapers, so I suspect he was quite in touch with what was going on.
But we did not sort of sit down and have discussions.
I think there were all a series of sort of gut reactions to immediate things that were happening, such as having our windows smashed early.
But we did not have strategy sessions.
I know during that time my parents made desperate efforts to get out, for our whole family to get out.
We applied to relatives in the United States.
And we couldn't get anybody to sponsor us.
So a good deal of the atmosphere in the '30s for us was the attempt to get out and not being able to, of our parents making effort.
But we did not have sort of strategy sessions of how to cope with life in the community, or how I would cope with the school situation.
I don't remember discussing it, talking about it with my parents.
Well, there were basically two.
The first one I've already mentioned, around 1934.
And then the second one was the Kristallnacht.
The Kristallnacht is a date that stands out in my mind because it was also my birthday night.
The night of my birthday was the night of the Kristallnacht.
That is, I was born on November 10th and the-- the events took place on the 9th to the 10th, so I couldn't very well ignore it.
But during the daytime on the 9th, suddenly a lot of cars came from outside.
There were no-- nobody owned cars in my village.
So cars came up and people came out who arrested all the Jewish men, including my brother.
My brother was then 17.
So all the Jewish men were suddenly taken away.
I mean, we had heard about events that led up to Kristallnacht in terms that a German embassy official in France had been assassinated by a Jew.
And so we knew there were things in the air, but we didn't know what was happening.
So during the daytime, all of a sudden all the Jewish men were arrested and we were terribly scared.
And my mother and I-- my sister had already left for America and my brother had left.
My brother was taken away.
So my mother and I went to the neighbor house and-- where there was the girl, Margot, and her mother, and we hid in the attic, in the evening, in their attic.

And then during the night, we heard cars come up and people starting smashing buildings and smashing the front door of the place in the house where I lived, where I was hiding upstairs in the attic, and smashing everything.
And then we also heard them-- since our house was right next door, we heard them smashing our house, lots of noise and broken glass and people yelling, where are the Jews?
So we were in terror, wondering are they going to find us and kill us.
So we stayed 'til the next morning.
I mean, it got quiet after a few-- what seemed like a few hours.
And in the morning, we crawled out and over rubble.
All the windows were smashed.
All the doors were smashed.
The front doors were smashed.
The furniture was smashed.
We had this German furniture with a high glass fronts.
These were all smashed, the glass smashed, toppled over.
And bedding with-- thick German bedding, well, these were all ripped open and stuff all over, feathers, feather bedding and all that.
So both in the house of our neighbors-- the Gruenfelds was their name-- and our house was rubble, doors smashed, and so we climbed over rubble.
So one of the striking things at that point for me was none of the Christian families came.
Nobody came.
We were totally alone.
And it was the women who were taking care of things, my mother in this case.
But apparently not all behaved exactly the same way.
The woman across the street from us, a Jewish woman who was-- had a very different personality from my mother.
When-- she didn't hide, she stayed in her house.
And when the Nazis came, she opened the door and said, come in, gentleman.
And they came in and they didn't smash up her house.
And this lady eventually saved her family.
They got out.
My mother died.
And so from a child's perspective, we always disliked that woman.
She was this aggressive, masculine woman.
And yet she knew how to handle the situation far better than my sweet, warm, loving mother.
But anyway we were totally alone with this rubble.
And my mother sort of fixed things up as best she could, clearing rubble and so on.
And also, the synagogue had been desecrated with the Torahs thrown onto heaps of manure.
Remember, this was a farming village.
There were manure piles not far away.
So in some ways, this hurt me more than the desecration of my house.
No.
To my knowledge, not one, not one.
They always claimed it was outsiders.
No.
To my knowledge.
I might be wrong.
I was not aware that any non-Jews came to our assistance.
They all became very distant.
They separated themselves.
So what happened then, the men were gone.
We didn't know where they were.
And we found out after about a week or so that they were-- that some of the men were in sort of a regional I guess you'd call it police lockup.
I don't know in which town, but within the same county.
And some of the men were sent to concentration camps.
My father and brother were sent to that regional place.
As far as I know, as far as I could guess, the ones who had no belongings left worth anything were kept in the regional jail.
And the ones who still had something were sent to concentration camp.
Notably, one who was a fairly large farmer, who owned a fairly large farm, and I think he stayed there until he signed his farm away is my recollection.
It might or might not be true.
Anyway, after-- I remember we were allowed to visit my father and my brother in the-- after some weeks my mother and I going there.
I don't know how we went there.
There must been some sort of-- there were public buses that came through our village.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We had no other transportation.
We had bicycles.
So anyway, after-- again, I'm really guessing-- some two months or so, my father and brother came back.
The people in the concentration camp, including that farmer and the baker next door, who was also sent to concentration camp, came back after some months.
And the baker, when he came back, I couldn't recognize him.
Why?
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I can't-- I couldn't recognize this man.
First of all, he had no hair.
He wasn't bald the way I am.
He had hair when he left and he had none-- He was shaven, gaunt.
His-- his cheeks collapsed, sort of gaunt-looking man, and deep eyes, as though a ghost came.
And so anyway, what happened in his case was that [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] when the next roundup came, he committed suicide.
So the pressure to-- I mean, at that point, it was clear that there was no future for us, for Jews, for us.
And so we redoubled our efforts to get out.
And I think at that point, I suspect my parents made the decision that if they couldn't get out, they would at least try to get me out.
That's-- I mean, I don't remember somebody saying this, but I felt that was the decision, that-- I mean, we wrote to everybody we could think of about trying to sponsor us.
And my parents didn't have the contacts to get out.
We had relatives in America, the ones who'd sponsored my sister.
But they themselves were new immigrants and extremely poor.
I know they were desperately trying to get us out and they failed.
So anyway, I suspect that that point was sort of the turning point in terms of saying, at all costs we must save whoever can be saved.
So my parents apparently, particularly my mother, must've found out about the children's movement in England that rescued children.
And so sometime after that-- so during the year 1939-- so Kristallnacht was in November '38.
So in the next few months, they must have made arrangements.
They must have heard about the Kindertransport, the English rescue organization.
Where do you think your mother heard about it?
I don't know.
Well, that brings to mind a possible contact.
My-- my parents' financial situation went from desperate to disastrous, that is no income.
And so my mother ended up doing cleaning work for a rabbi some-- in some other community.
And I suspect she might have heard about it there.
I'm not sure.
I suspect she probably heard about it there, through those contacts.

So the business of my mother having to do cleaning, house cleaning, when we ourselves had had a maid at one time, meant our circumstances financially and economically went way down and I felt very badly about it.
But I suspect that's where my mother heard.
But I'm not sure.
I mean, we're talking about life in a village.
It was rather isolated.
We didn't have access to the sort of information that people had in cities.
But it did reach us, at least this information.
They must have heard about the children's organization in England.
Well, in retrospect, amazingly little.
I had-- I mean, I was badgering my parents day and night that I wanted to get out, that I want to get away.
I was badgering them day in, day out, begging them, pleading.
And that was sort of a prominent memory I carried with me for many years that I left them.
I left them behind.
But anyway, to answer your question, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it strikes me that I didn't concern myself with where I was going, even the matter of the English language.
I didn't speak English, didn't know any English.
And who would take care of me?
I had no sense at all and it didn't concern me.
I had just one priority, one issue.
I wanted to get away.
That is all I could think of, day and night.
So then when it finally came to going away, my parents did hear that this was going to be to England.
And they took me to a priest who knew some English, who gave me-- I think I had two lessons in English.
And I remember it was sort of a strange situation.
The priest was in the next village.
And he-- I remember sort of a creepy house.
But anyway, he must have taught me a few words.
I-- I don't think it was more than two lessons.
So anyway, when it came time for going away, my mother took me to a train, the nearest train.
I don't remember where she took me, frankly.
I remember her taking me to the train.
I don't know whether it was to Frankfurt or to a nearer town.
But I remember her-- I remember my mother taking me.
My father didn't have the money to come along, so I said goodbye to him at the house.
I don't remember embracing him or what, how I said goodbye.
But I do remember he wasn't able to come along to the train.
We went by bus to the train.
I had one suitcase.
And my mother took me to the train.
She stood outside the train and handed me over.
Apparently this was a train that was transporting a whole number of children, perhaps 30.
I have no idea really how many.
And it stopped in that city.
I think it was Frankfurt.
My mother put me on the train.
I remember embracing her and she was crying.
And I sat by the window and looked at her and waved at her.
But I had no sense of who was taking care of me.
There was somebody in charge of the children on the train, but I didn't know this person beforehand.
I didn't-- I never knew her name.
And I had no sense of where we were going, because all I could think of, I want to go away.
Well, I have a watch that I can show a little later.
And I had some silver spoons, which I got on my-- my godfather gave me a silver spoon.
An uncle of mine who died in the Holocaust gave me a silver spoon on the anniversary of my birthday.
I have two of those.
That's it.
Oh, I have prayer books.
Yes.
I have Jewish prayer books that I will show a bit later.
And the clothes, my mother made most of my clothes, so most of these early clothes I had were things that my mother made.
My mother was a good seamstress.
She was not a very good cook, so in a sense I don't know how Jewish she was, if she wasn't good-- but she wasn't a very good cook.
And eating was not a big thing in our family.
It was not part of our emphasis.
But anyway, so I took with me these items [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] without really confronting the issue of where I was going, who was going to take care of me.
I think I had to have kind of a passport.
It was not an adult passport.
It was sort of a special children's permission to leave the country.
Yes.
I had that.
And my parents arranged for that.
No.
And given my personality, as far as I know, I simply sat like that and didn't-- I suspect I never talked to anybody all night.
It was in the-- somehow we traveled into the night and we traveled to Holland.
And from Holland, we took an overnight ship to England.
As far as I know, I didn't talk to anybody and I was the only one from my village who went.
I don't remember.
I don't remember.
So it was at night.
And I just remember sort of confusion of getting on-- we were there for seemed like two or three hours or so on a-- on a port and getting on a ship.
I mean, we knew-- I knew we were going to England.
Well, I'm guessing about 35.
But I'm-- I'm really unsure, and I don't know where they came from.
I know nothing.
I don't remember anything about it.
And so incidentally, the date, I think it was sometime in July or August 1939 that this-- that I went to England.
I think there was just one.
But I-- I don't remember whether it was a man or a woman.
I was totally oblivious to the world.
And I guess I was sort of in my own little world of fear.
And-- I mean, this sort of is my sense of myself, just inward, and not in a sense of looking out, where am I going kind of thing.
But that was in part perhaps my particular personality all through childhood.
I was that sort of a person, perhaps very similar to my father.
But I don't remember how many people there were, who was taking care of us, who sponsored us, nothing.
Bumpy.
Bumpy to England.
Well, again we were sort of herded onto a train to London.
I think it was to London.
And somehow night and day merge.
I don't-- I think we were-- it was all an overnight thing.
We arrived in the morning.
But I don't remember being fed or-- the physical side of it is totally dissipated.
It's gone somehow.
So I mean, I guess there's an envelope of fear around me in which I lived, a cocoon of fear.
And that was my little world.
And so there were-- I was willing to be led around and pushed and pulled.
So we-- as a group, we went to London and somehow we were then shipped off or took another train ride to some kind of a coastal camp for about two weeks.
I believe it was Margate in England.
But I-- I don't have my information very straight.
So we were there for about two weeks.
And then we were sent back to London to sort of a boarding school for about a month.
And we were in a boarding situation in some kind of a house-- I remember four of us in one room-- and attended school during the daytime.
There I do remember one boy, Manfred Schoenfeld.
I don't know what ever became of him.
He was in the bed next to me.
He was very nice, a roly-poly jolly fat fellow.
And everybody liked him and I liked him.
So there, we developed some-- some fraternal warmth, friendly interaction.
I think there were only boys in this school situation.
So we were there for about a month and going to some sort of a school.

Then most of us were sent to a school where I was going to stay for the next four years, Bunce Court School in Kent, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] which was near the village of Otterden, O-T-T-E-R-D-E-N, in Kent, near Faversham, which is slightly bigger community in Kent.
So that was a very unusual school.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it-- it-- well, it was unusual for the following reason.
In the 1920s, a Jewish woman in Germany became interested in studying education in America.
And she went to America to study education at Columbia University.
There was a John Dewey progressive education thing.
She studied education and became very much involved with the Quakers.
So she went back to Germany and established what was then a very progressive co-educational boarding school near Herrlingen in Germany.

And it was the new Herrlingen School that she established on very progressive basis of-- and then when Hitler came to power in 1934, 1933, shortly thereafter she moved the whole school to England, where it-- What was her name?
Her name was Anna Essinger, E-S-S-I-N-G-E-R.
So she established a school in England.
And it was unusual in that we had no servants.
The school was run-- there were teachers and there was a hired cook.
But all the physical upkeep was done by the teachers and the children.
And boys and girls lived there.
All the cleaning and everything was done by the staff and children.
It was a very egalitarian community.
And we didn't emphasize classical learning.
Instead of learning the classics, we learned how to grow potatoes and vegetables.
Bunce Court.
School.
Sort of a camp.
I mean, I'm not accustomed to American type of camps.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Well, I didn't have an overwhelming sense of I have now arrived.
I was just being bounced to yet another place.
And so I think people were trying to make us feel welcome.
The people were friendly.
And many-- most of the teachers were themselves German Jewish immigrants.
There were also some English teachers.
And there were some English children.
But most of us there were refugees.
So in a sense, we were-- we had a great deal in common in terms of where we came from and so on.
So the-- there was a deliberate attempt to provide a home.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But it didn't always succeed and my memory of it was that I was not receptive.
I was not available to being hugged.
I was not available to being loved.
I was terribly scared and inward and I probably pushed people away.
But anyway, my-- my memory of the school is there were good intentions, but nobody was really available to me.
The memory I have is that nobody-- during the years that I was there, nobody touched my face.
Nobody ever said, what do you want?
Although, at first when we came there, I think they wanted to be friendly, but it didn't work.
What was a typical day like?
Well, we were very egalitarian.
We went to school, to classes together, but we also worked together.
So there was a very egalitarian thing of working together.
In one sense, that has stayed with me, an egalitarian attitude about life.
